devanagari: निमिवंशवर्णनंनिमेर्देहत्यागो विदेहवंशश्च । श्रीशुक उवाच । निमिरिक्ष्वाकुतनयो वसिष्ठं अवृतर्त्विजम् । आरभ्य सत्रं सोऽप्याह शक्रेण प्राग्व...
english_devnagari: nimivaṃśavarṇanaṃnimerdehatyāgo videhavaṃśaścaśrīśuka uvācanimirikṣvākutanayo vasiṣṭhaṃ avṛtartvijamārabhya satraṃ so'pyāha śakreṇa prāgvṛto'smi bhoḥ
translation:
Śrī Śuka continued:
After commencing the session of a sacrifice, Nimi, the son of Ikṣvāku invited Vasiṣṭha to officiate as a sacrificial priest (ṛtvij). He, on his part, replied, “Oh king! I have been already requested by Indra (to conduct his sacrifice).

devanagari: तं निर्वर्त्यागमिष्यामि तावन्मां प्रतिपालय । तूष्णीं आसीद् गृहपतिः सोऽपीन्द्रस्याकरोन् मखम् ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: taṃ nirvartyāgamiṣyāmi tāvanmāṃ pratipālayatūṣṇīṃ āsīd gṛhapatiḥ so'pīndrasyākaron makham
translation: After completing his sacrifice, I shall return (to look after your sacrifice). Wait till then for me”. The house-holder (Nimi, the performer of the sacrifice) kept silent. He (Vasiṣṭha) finished the sacrifice of Indra.

devanagari: निमिश्चलमिदं विद्वान् सत्रमारभतामात्मवान् । ऋत्विग्भिः अपरैस्तावत् नागमद्यावता गुरुः ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: nimiścalamidaṃ vidvān satramārabhatāmātmavānṛtvigbhiḥ aparaistāvat nāgamadyāvatā guruḥ
translation: Nimi, himself a thoughtful philosopher, knew how life is transitory and proceeded with the sacrifice with the help of other sacrificial priests, while his preceptor did not return (was absent).

devanagari: शिष्यव्यतिक्रमं वीक्ष्य तं निर्वर्त्य गुरुरागतः । अशपत् पतताद् देहो निमेः पण्डितमानिनः ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: śiṣyavyatikramaṃ vīkṣya taṃ nirvartya gururāgataḥaśapat patatād deho nimeḥ paṇḍitamāninaḥ
translation: The preceptor returned after concluding Indra’s sacrifice. Perceiving this disobedience on the part of his disciple, he cursed: ‘Let the body of Nimi who conceitedly regards himself as a learned man, fall’.

devanagari: निमिः प्रतिददौ शापं गुरवेऽधर्मवर्तिने । तवापि पतताद् देहो लोभाद् धर्ममजानतः ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: nimiḥ pratidadau śāpaṃ gurave'dharmavartinetavāpi patatād deho lobhād dharmamajānataḥ
translation: Nimi reciprocated the imprecation of his preceptor who violated the path of righteousness: “May your body fall, as you do not know what is dharma due to covetousness”.

devanagari: इत्युत्ससर्ज स्वं देहं निमिरध्यात्मकोविदः । मित्रावरुणयोर्जज्ञे उर्वश्यां प्रपितामहः ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: ityutsasarja svaṃ dehaṃ nimiradhyātmakovidaḥmitrāvaruṇayorjajñe urvaśyāṃ prapitāmahaḥ
translation: With these words Nimi, who was proficient in spiritual lore cast off his body. My great grand-father[1] Vasiṣṭha was born of Urvaśī from Mitra and Varuṇa.

devanagari: गन्धवस्तुषु तद् देहं निधाय मुनिसत्तमाः । समाप्ते सत्रयागेऽथ देवान् ऊचुः समागतान् ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: gandhavastuṣu tad dehaṃ nidhāya munisattamāḥsamāpte satrayāge'tha devān ūcuḥ samāgatān
translation: Preserving Nimi’s body in fragrant balms, the great sages, at the conclusion of the sacrificial session, spoke to the celestials who assembled there for the sacrifice.

devanagari: राज्ञो जीवतु देहोऽयं प्रसन्नाः प्रभवो यदि । तथेत्युक्ते निमिः प्राह मा भून्मे देहबन्धनम् ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: rājño jīvatu deho'yaṃ prasannāḥ prabhavo yaditathetyukte nimiḥ prāha mā bhūnme dehabandhanam
translation: “If ye Lords are (propitiated and) pleased, the body of the king be resuscitated”. “Amen! Be it so”, said the gods. The soul of Nimi resorted to his liṅga-śarīra and spoke loudly, “Let me have no bondage of the physical body”.

devanagari: यस्य योगं न वाञ्छन्ति वियोगभयकातराः । भजन्ति चरणाम्भोजं मुनयो हरिमेधसः ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: yasya yogaṃ na vāñchanti viyogabhayakātarāḥbhajanti caraṇāmbhojaṃ munayo harimedhasaḥ
translation: Being afraid of separation from it, sages do not wish to have any contact (or union) with the body, but they devote themselves to the lotus-like feet of Hari (to escape from Saṃsāra).

devanagari: देहं नावरुरुत्सेऽहं दुःखशोकभयावहम् । सर्वत्रास्य यतो मृत्युं मत्स्यानां उदके यथा ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: dehaṃ nāvarurutse'haṃ duḥkhaśokabhayāvahamsarvatrāsya yato mṛtyuṃ matsyānāṃ udake yathā
translation: I am not desirous of re-enteriṇg into the body which brings misery, grief and fear. It is because of the physical body that the death threatens the jīva on all sides and the peril of death is everywhere, as the fish have water on all sides.

devanagari: देवा ऊचुः । विदेह उष्यतां कामं लोचनेषु शरीरिणाम् । उन्मेषणनिमेषाभ्यां लक्षितोऽध्यात्मसंस्थितः ॥ ११ ॥
english_devnagari: devā ūcuḥvideha uṣyatāṃ kāmaṃ locaneṣu śarīriṇāmunmeṣaṇanimeṣābhyāṃ lakṣito'dhyātmasaṃsthitaḥ
translation:
Gods said:
“Oh incorporal Nimi! May you live at your free will in the eyes of embodied beings.” Nimi thus took his place in the bodies of creatures, and is indicated by the opening and closing of eyelids.

devanagari: अराजकभयं नॄणां मन्यमाना महर्षयः । देहं ममन्थुः स्म निमेः कुमारः समजायत ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: arājakabhayaṃ nṝṇāṃ manyamānā maharṣayaḥdehaṃ mamanthuḥ sma nimeḥ kumāraḥ samajāyata
translation: Considering the fearful consequences of anarchy on the human beings, the great sages churned the body of Nimi and a son was born of him.

devanagari: इति श्रीमद्‍भागवते महापुराणे पारमहंस्यां । संहितायां नवमस्कन्धे त्रयोदशोऽध्यायः ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: iti śrīmad‍bhāgavate mahāpurāṇe pāramahaṃsyāṃsaṃhitāyāṃ navamaskandhe trayodaśo'dhyāyaḥ
translation: On account of this miraculous birth (or by being the founder of a dynasty), he became known as Janaka and was called “Vaideha” as he was produced from a life-less body or an incorporal being, and ‘Mithila’ (‘Mithi’ in other Purāṇas) as he got his birth out of churning (of Nimi’s body). And the city of Mithilā was built up by him.[2]
purport:
Footnote [2]:
Cf. Vālmīki Rāmāyaṇa.—Uttara 57.19 & 20.
mathanān mithir ityāhur jananāj janako'bhavat /19
yasmād videhāt sambhūto Vaidehas tu tataḥ smṛtaḥ /20

devanagari: जन्मना जनकः सोऽभूद् वैदेहस्तु विदेहजः । मिथिलो मथनाज्जातो मिथिला येन निर्मिता । तस्माद् उदावसुस्तस्य पुत्रोऽभूत् नन्दिवर्धनः । ततः सुके...
english_devnagari: janmanā janakaḥ so'bhūd vaidehastu videhajaḥmithilo mathanājjāto mithilā yena nirmitātasmād udāvasustasya putro'bhūt nandivardhanaḥtataḥ suketustasyāpi devarāto mahīpate
translation: Janaka’s son was Udāvasu of whom was born Nandivardhana; his son was Suketu (Sukeśa) who gave birth to Devarāta, Oh King!

devanagari: तस्माद् बृहद्रथस्तस्य महावीर्यः सुधृत्पिता । सुधृतेर्धृष्टकेतुर्वै हर्यश्वोऽथ मरुस्ततः ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: tasmād bṛhadrathastasya mahāvīryaḥ sudhṛtpitāsudhṛterdhṛṣṭaketurvai haryaśvo'tha marustataḥ
translation: From him was born Bṛhadratha, whose son was Mahāvīrya, the father of Sudhṛt; from him sprang up Dhṛṣṭaketu; his son was Haryaśva, whose son was Maru.

devanagari: मरोः प्रतीपकस्तस्मात् जातः कृतरथो यतः । देवमीढस्तस्य पुत्रो विश्रुतोऽथ महाधृतिः ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: maroḥ pratīpakastasmāt jātaḥ kṛtaratho yataḥdevamīḍhastasya putro viśruto'tha mahādhṛtiḥ
translation: Maru’s son was Pratīpaka whence was born Kṛti; his son was Devamīḍha whose son was Viśruta, the father of Mahādhṛti.

devanagari: कृतिरातः ततस्तस्मात् महारोमा च तत्सुतः । स्वर्णरोमा सुतस्तस्य ह्रस्वरोमा व्यजायत ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: kṛtirātaḥ tatastasmāt mahāromā ca tatsutaḥsvarṇaromā sutastasya hrasvaromā vyajāyata
translation: His son was Kṛtirāta whose son was Mahāromā; his son was Svarṇaromā from whom was born Hrasvaromā.

devanagari: ततः शीरध्वजो जज्ञे यज्ञार्थं कर्षतो महीम् । सीता शीराग्रतो जाता तस्मात् सीरध्वजः स्मृतः ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: tataḥ śīradhvajo jajñe yajñārthaṃ karṣato mahīmsītā śīrāgrato jātā tasmāt sīradhvajaḥ smṛtaḥ
translation: From him sprang up Sīradhvaja, who while ploughing the earth for sacrifice, Sītā (the consort of Rāma) was born (discovered) at the point of the ploughshare. He is hence remembered as Sīradhvaja[3] (one whose plough gave him celebrity like a flag).
purport:
Footnote [3]:
Bhāvārtha-dīpikā-prakāśa quotes a legend from Adbhuta Rāmāyaṇa, according to which Sītā was originally in the womb of Mandodari who deposited the foetus in the Kurukṣetra (?) and was discovered by Hrasvaromā.

devanagari: कुशध्वजस्तस्य पुत्रः ततो धर्मध्वजो नृपः । धर्मध्वजस्य द्वौ पुत्रौ कृतध्वजमितध्वजौ ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: kuśadhvajastasya putraḥ tato dharmadhvajo nṛpaḥdharmadhvajasya dvau putrau kṛtadhvajamitadhvajau
translation: From him was born Kuśadhvaja, whose son was Dharmadhvaja. He (Dharma) had two sons called Kṛtadhvaja and Mitadhvaja.

devanagari: कृतध्वजात् केशिध्वजः खाण्डिक्यस्तु मितध्वजात् । कृतध्वजसुतो राजन् आत्मविद्याविशारदः ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: kṛtadhvajāt keśidhvajaḥ khāṇḍikyastu mitadhvajātkṛtadhvajasuto rājan ātmavidyāviśāradaḥ
translation: From Kṛtadhvaja, Keśidhvaja, and from Mitadhvaja, Khāṇḍikya were born. The son of Kṛtadhvaja was expert in the spiritual lore, Oh King.

devanagari: खाण्डिक्यः कर्मतत्त्वज्ञो भीतः केशिध्वजाद् द्रुतः । भानुमांस्तस्य पुत्रोऽभूत् शतद्युम्नस्तु तत्सुतः ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: khāṇḍikyaḥ karmatattvajño bhītaḥ keśidhvajād drutaḥbhānumāṃstasya putro'bhūt śatadyumnastu tatsutaḥ
translation: Khāṇḍikya was proficient in the path of action (karma-mārga), but being afraid of Keśidhvaja, he ran away (leaving his country), Keśidhvaja had a son called Bhānumān whose son was Sudyumna.

devanagari: शुचिस्तु तनयस्तस्मात् सनद्वाजः सुतोऽभवत् । ऊर्जकेतुः सनद्वाजाद् अजोऽथ पुरुजित्सुतः ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: śucistu tanayastasmāt sanadvājaḥ suto'bhavatūrjaketuḥ sanadvājād ajo'tha purujitsutaḥ
translation: His son was Śuci, whose son was Sanadvāja; from Sanadvāja was born Ūrdhvaketu whose son was Aja, who had Purujit as his son.

devanagari: अरिष्टनेमिस्तस्यापि श्रुतायुः तत्सुपार्श्वकः । ततश्चित्ररथो यस्य क्षेमाधिर्मिथिलाधिपः ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: ariṣṭanemistasyāpi śrutāyuḥ tatsupārśvakaḥtataścitraratho yasya kṣemādhirmithilādhipaḥ
translation: His son was Ariṣṭanemi whose son was Śrutāyu; his son Supārśvaka gave birth to Citraratha, whose son Kṣemardhi was the king of Mithilā.

devanagari: तस्मात् समरथस्तस्य सुतः सत्यरथस्ततः । आसीद् उपगुरुस्तस्माद् उपगुप्तोऽग्निसंभवः ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: tasmāt samarathastasya sutaḥ satyarathastataḥāsīd upagurustasmād upagupto'gnisaṃbhavaḥ
translation: From him Samaratha whose son was Satyaratha; from him sprang Vpaguru whose son was Upagupta who was an aṃśa of the Fire-god.

devanagari: वस्वनन्तोऽथ तत्पुत्रो युयुधो यत् सुभाषणः । श्रुतस्ततो जयस्तस्माद् विजयोऽस्मादृतः सुतः ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: vasvananto'tha tatputro yuyudho yat subhāṣaṇaḥśrutastato jayastasmād vijayo'smādṛtaḥ sutaḥ
translation: His son was Vasvananta, whose son was Yuyudha, from whom was born Subhāṣaṇa; his son was Śruta whose son was Jaya, the father of Vijaya. His son was Ṛta.

devanagari: शुनकस्तत्सुतो जज्ञे वीतहव्यो धृतिस्ततः । बहुलाश्वो धृतेस्तस्य कृतिरस्य महावशी ॥ २६ ॥
english_devnagari: śunakastatsuto jajñe vītahavyo dhṛtistataḥbahulāśvo dhṛtestasya kṛtirasya mahāvaśī
translation: From him was born Śunaka, whose son was Vītahavya, from whom was born Dhṛti. From Dhṛti was born Bahulāśva, whose son was Kṛti noted for great self-control.

devanagari: एते वै मैथिला राजन् आत्मविद्याविशारदाः । योगेश्वरप्रसादेन द्वन्द्वैर्मुक्ता गृहेष्वपि ॥ २७ ॥
english_devnagari: ete vai maithilā rājan ātmavidyāviśāradāḥyogeśvaraprasādena dvandvairmuktā gṛheṣvapi
translation:
These are verily the kings of Mithilā who were all proficient in the Science of the Soul (Spiritual lore) through the grace of Yājñavalkya the master of yoga. Even though they led the life of householders, they were above the conflicting pairs like pleasure and pain, etc.
[back to top]
